# Integrative metabolism: the fed and fasted body

> *This is where isolated nutrients become a working system. The first-year goal is
> **recognition**: read the metabolic map as one connected network, then understand how
> hormones throw it between **storing** after a meal and **mobilising** between meals — for
> the brain's sake above all. Not enzyme kinetics, not diabetes management — the logic, not
> every reaction.*

## The body as one system: what this chapter does {#sec-overview}

Chapter 3 sorted food into macronutrients; Chapter 5 followed a meal from the plate into the
blood. This chapter answers the question those two raise: **once the nutrients are in, what
does the body do with them — and how does it survive the hours and days between meals?**

The answer comes in **two layers**, and we build them in order.

- **Part A — the metabolic map.** A handful of core pathways and, more importantly, *how they
  connect*. We teach this from scratch: this book does not assume a separate biochemistry
  course. But we stay at **map depth** — each pathway as *what goes in, what comes out, where
  in the cell it happens, and its one named control step* — never the full enzyme machinery.
  The goal is a student who can read the map and predict where carbon and energy flow, not one
  who recites reactions.
- **Part B — the map, alive.** The same map, now driven by hormones across the states a body
  actually lives in: **just fed, overnight-fasted, a day without food, prolonged starvation,
  and exercise** — closing on what a *broken* switch looks like (diabetes), and on the honest
  physiology behind Ramadan, intermittent fasting and keto.

A single idea runs through both layers: **metabolism is not a list of separate topics to
memorise — it is one network with a logic.** Learn the logic and most of the detail follows.

---

# Part A — reading the metabolic map {#sec-partA}

## Five crossroads and four one-way streets {#sec-map}

Metabolism looks impossible when you meet it as fifty enzymes. It becomes navigable when you
notice that almost everything passes through just **five crossroads** — hub molecules where
pathways meet and carbon can turn several ways:

1. **Glucose-6-phosphate (G6P)** — the carbohydrate roundabout.
2. **Pyruvate** — the three-way junction at the end of glucose breakdown.
3. **Acetyl-CoA** — the universal funnel where *all three* fuels converge.
4. **The TCA cycle** (built around oxaloacetate) — the central furnace.
5. **The amino-acid pool** — where protein enters and leaves the map.

Two ideas apply everywhere on this map. First, the difference between **catabolism** (breaking
fuel down — pathways *converge* on acetyl-CoA and release energy) and **anabolism** (building
and storing — pathways *diverge*, and they cost energy). Second, **reciprocal regulation**:
opposing pathways are never both fully switched on at once, or they would cancel each other out
in a pointless "futile cycle." Making glucose and burning glucose, building fat and burning fat
— each pair is coordinated so that one runs while its opposite idles.

Finally, the map has **direction**, and the direction comes from **four one-way streets** —
irreversible steps that carbon can pass through but never come back. One of them, as we will
see, is the single most important fact in the whole chapter. Everything else is reversible
give-and-take; these four valves are why metabolism flows the way it does.

Build the map yourself below — one pathway at a time onto the fixed backbone of the five
crossroads. Watch where each new pathway *snaps on*: that convergence is the whole point.

<div data-island="metabolic-map" data-src="/diagrams/metabolic-map.yml"></div>

## The carbohydrate roundabout: glucose-6-phosphate {#sec-g6p}

**A glucose molecule entering a cell is immediately trapped** — tagged with a phosphate to make
**glucose-6-phosphate**, which cannot leak back out. From this one roundabout, carbon can leave
by four exits, and learning just this node makes the carbohydrate map fall into place:

- **Burn it** — down **glycolysis** to pyruvate (for energy).
- **Store it** — into **glycogen**, the body's fast-access glucose bank.
- **Build with it** — into the **pentose phosphate pathway**, which yields not ATP but **NADPH**
  (a currency for building fats and defending against oxidative damage) and the ribose sugar for
  DNA and RNA.
- **Export it** — release free glucose to the blood. Crucially, **only the liver and kidney can
  take this exit**; most cells cannot. Remember this — it is the hinge of Part B.

**Glycolysis** — splitting glucose into two **pyruvate** — happens in the cell's **cytosol** and,
importantly, **does not need oxygen**. Its committed control step is the enzyme
**phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)**. It yields a little ATP directly. When oxygen is scarce (a
sprinting muscle, or a red blood cell that has no mitochondria at all), pyruvate is converted to
**lactate**, and this reaction regenerates the molecule glycolysis needs to keep running — so a
cell can make ATP with no oxygen at all. Oxygen is used later, and elsewhere (§@sec-furnace) —
*not* in glycolysis itself. This is the single most common misconception about glucose breakdown.

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: why anaerobic exercise burns sugar, not fat
Sprint up a flight of stairs and your muscles run on glucose, not fat — because only glucose can
be broken down *without* oxygen, fast, right there in the cytosol. Fat can only be burned
aerobically, which is slower. That is why a 100 m dash is fuelled by carbohydrate and a long
walk increasingly by fat — a distinction we return to under exercise (§@sec-exercise)
[@statpearlsAnaerobic].
:::

## Pyruvate, and the door that only opens one way {#sec-pyruvate}

**Pyruvate** sits at the end of glycolysis as a genuine junction. Its carbon can go four ways: to
**lactate** (regenerating what glycolysis needs, as above); to **alanine** (into the amino-acid
pool); *back up* toward glucose via **oxaloacetate** (the first step of making new glucose); or
— the fateful one — forward into **acetyl-CoA**.

That last step, **pyruvate → acetyl-CoA**, is catalysed by **pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH)**, and
it is **irreversible. There is no way back.** This is the first and most important of the map's
four one-way streets, and it carries a consequence out of all proportion to its size:

::: {.callout-important}
## The one fact to carry out of Part A
Because pyruvate → acetyl-CoA is a one-way door, and **fat is broken down into acetyl-CoA**,
**fat cannot be turned back into glucose.** (The only exception is the small **glycerol**
backbone of a triglyceride — see §@sec-fat.) This single valve is *why* the body must so
carefully defend and remake glucose when food runs short. Hold onto it: the whole of Part B
turns on it.
:::

## Making glucose, and banking it {#sec-glucose-store}

If glucose cannot be made from fat, where does it come from when the diet stops supplying it? Two
sources, in order.

**Glycogen — the fast buffer.** Glucose is stored as **glycogen**, a branched chain of glucose
units, in liver and muscle. Building it (glycogenesis) and breaking it down (glycogenolysis) are
reciprocally controlled, so the bank is either filling or emptying, not both. Glycogen is the
*short-term* account — enough for hours, not days.

**Gluconeogenesis — making glucose from scratch.** When glycogen runs low, the liver (and, later,
the kidney) **builds new glucose** from three kinds of raw material: **lactate**, **glycerol**,
and **glucogenic amino acids**. A common trap is to picture this as "glycolysis run backwards."
It is not: three of glycolysis's steps are irreversible, so gluconeogenesis must **bypass them
using four different enzymes** — three more of the map's one-way streets. Those bypass enzymes
are its control points, and because glycolysis and gluconeogenesis are reciprocally regulated,
the cell is never wastefully doing both at once. This chapter names the *raw materials*; the
enzyme steps belong to biochemistry proper.

## Fat: the densest fuel, and its on/off switch {#sec-fat}

Fat is the body's **energy-dense** fuel — about **9 kcal/g against roughly 4** for carbohydrate
or protein — which is exactly why it is the form the body uses for long-term storage.

**Burning fat.** A fatty acid is carried into the mitochondrion by a shuttle whose gate,
**CPT1**, is the committed control step; inside, **β-oxidation** chops it into **many molecules
of acetyl-CoA** (plus the electron-carriers that feed energy production). Many acetyl-CoA per
fatty acid is why fat yields so much energy.

**Storing fat.** In the other direction, the enzyme **acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC)** commits
acetyl-CoA to building fatty acids (a process that consumes the **NADPH** made back at the
pentose phosphate pathway). The switch between the two is beautifully simple, and a textbook case
of reciprocal regulation: **ACC's immediate product blocks CPT1.** So when the cell is building
fat, it has automatically shut the door on burning it. You do not build and burn fat at the same
time.

**Ketone bodies.** There is a third fate for acetyl-CoA, and it becomes vital in Part B. When fat
breakdown floods the fasting **liver** with more acetyl-CoA than it can burn, the liver converts
the surplus into **ketone bodies** and exports them for other organs to use. The liver makes them
but — lacking the enzyme (**SCOT**) to use them — **cannot burn its own product.** It is a ketone
factory that ships its entire output. Keep that oddity in mind; it is the key to how the body
survives a long fast.

## The central furnace: the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation {#sec-furnace}

Acetyl-CoA — no matter whether it came from carbohydrate, fat, or protein — enters the **TCA
cycle** (also called the citric acid or Krebs cycle) in the **mitochondrion**. There it is
fully oxidised to **CO₂**, and the energy is captured onto electron-carrier molecules. Two things
make the TCA cycle the heart of the map. It is the **final common pathway for all three fuels**,
not just carbohydrate; and it is **amphibolic** — its intermediates are also borrowed to build
other molecules and topped up from amino acids, so it is a supply depot as well as a furnace.

The electron-carriers then deliver their cargo to **oxidative phosphorylation** on the
mitochondrial inner membrane, and *this* is where the **bulk of the cell's ATP** is actually
made — and where **oxygen is finally used**, as the last acceptor of the spent electrons (which
is why you breathe). So the arithmetic of glucose comes out roughly like this: burned all the way
with oxygen, one glucose yields on the order of **30–32 ATP**; without oxygen, glycolysis alone
yields only about **2**. (Older textbooks say "36–38"; that figure is now considered an
overestimate. Treat all of these as *approximate* — the point is the ~15-fold gap between
aerobic and anaerobic, not a memorised number.)

## The energy currency: ATP is spent, not stored {#sec-atp}

It is tempting to think of ATP as the body's energy *store*. It is the opposite: **ATP is
currency, not savings.** You hold only seconds-to-minutes' worth at any moment, and you turn over
something close to your entire body weight in ATP over a day — making it and spending it
continuously. The body's actual energy *stores* are **glycogen and fat**, which are cashed into
ATP on demand. And note that not every carrier is an energy currency: **NADPH** (from the pentose
phosphate pathway) is a *building* currency, spent on synthesis and antioxidant defence, kept
deliberately separate from the ATP-generating carriers. Confusing the two is a common slip.

## The amino-acid pool: protein is a flow, not a store {#sec-amino}

The fifth crossroads is the **amino-acid pool**, and it comes with a warning that shapes all of
Part B: **there is no storage protein.** Unlike glycogen and fat, every amino acid in the body is
doing a job — as muscle, as an enzyme, as an immune cell. To use protein for fuel is therefore to
**dismantle working tissue**, which is why the body treats it as a late and costly resort, not a
first-line store.

To feed an amino acid into the map, its **nitrogen must be removed first** (by transamination —
the origin of the liver enzymes ALT and AST you will meet in the clinic — and deamination), and
that nitrogen is packaged as **urea** by the liver for the kidneys to excrete. The leftover carbon
skeleton then joins the map as one of two types:

- **Glucogenic** — enters as pyruvate or a TCA intermediate, and *can* become glucose. Most amino
  acids are at least partly glucogenic.
- **Ketogenic** — enters as acetyl-CoA, and (by the one-way-street rule) *cannot* become glucose,
  only ketones. **Leucine and lysine are the only two purely ketogenic amino acids** — the two
  worth remembering; several others are both, and the exact bookkeeping varies between textbooks
  and is not worth memorising here.

**Step back and see the logic.** The whole map is five crossroads, wired together by reversible
give-and-take, given its direction by four one-way streets. That is all Part A is. Everything in
Part B is this same map — with hormones deciding, meal by meal, which way the traffic runs.

---

# Part B — the map, alive: fed, fasted, starved {#sec-partB}

The pathways of Part A are always present in every cell. What *changes*, hour to hour, is which
of them are switched on — and that is set almost entirely by two hormones from the pancreas. The
interactive below is Part A's map "coming alive": step it from *just ate* through to *prolonged
fast* and watch which organs store, burn and export fuel, and how the traffic between them
changes. Most of Part B is in that one picture.

<div data-island="metabolic-switch" data-src="/diagrams/metabolic-switch.yml"></div>

## The master switch: the insulin-to-glucagon ratio {#sec-switch}

The body does not have a "fed hormone" and a "fasted hormone" acting alone. It reads a **ratio**:
**insulin against glucagon.** After a meal the ratio is **high** → *store*. Between meals it is
**low** → *mobilise*. The classic description is that this ratio "varies inversely with the need
to make your own glucose, and is lowest in starvation" [@unger1971ratio]. Learn the *direction*,
not a number.

The first correction to make is about insulin itself. Insulin is not "the sugar hormone." It is
the body's **master anabolic hormone**, acting on **all three** macronutrients at once: it stores
glucose (as glycogen), stores fat (and blocks its breakdown), and builds protein — while shutting
down the opposing, mobilising pathways [@merheb2026insulin]. *Insulin is the store-and-build
conductor; lowering blood glucose is only one thing it does.*

## The fed state: store everything {#sec-fed}

After a meal, insulin is high, and each major organ plays its part — this is integration, not new
pathways:

- **Liver** — the glucose buffer and hub. It takes up glucose, refills glycogen, converts the
  excess to fat and ships it out as **VLDL**, and receives the meal's amino acids arriving by the
  portal vein.
- **Skeletal muscle** — insulin's single biggest glucose sink. It takes up glucose to rebuild its
  own glycogen and takes up amino acids to build protein.
- **Adipose tissue** — the fat depot. It takes up glucose and stores fat; the enzyme
  **lipoprotein lipase** on its capillaries strips triglyceride out of circulating lipoproteins to
  stock the fat cell [@wang2009lpl]. And insulin is powerfully **anti-lipolytic** — it clamps the
  brakes on fat release.

::: {.callout-note}
## The clean integration point: GLUT4 versus GLUT2
Why does muscle and fat uptake *surge* after a meal, while the liver's does not? Because of the
**glucose door** each uses. Muscle and fat use **GLUT4**, which only comes to the cell surface
*when insulin tells it to* — so their uptake is switched on by the meal. The liver uses **GLUT2**,
which is always present and simply lets glucose in **in proportion to its concentration**, no
insulin required. Insulin still acts on the liver — but on its *enzymes*, telling it to store
rather than release, not on its glucose door [@chadt2020glucose]. This one contrast explains most
of the fed-state picture.
:::

## The fasted state: defend the blood glucose {#sec-fasted}

As blood glucose falls between meals, insulin drops and **glucagon** rises — the ratio flips to
*mobilise*. Glucagon leads a **counter-regulatory team** with a clear pecking order by speed:

- **Glucagon** — the primary, rapid responder; it drives the liver to release glucose.
- **Epinephrine (adrenaline)** — a fast backup, and the critical one if glucagon fails.
- **Cortisol** and **growth hormone** — slower and *permissive*, sustaining a prolonged fast
  rather than handling the acute dip.

The order matters clinically: the acute rescuers of a low blood glucose are **glucagon and
adrenaline**, not cortisol and growth hormone [@cryer1993counterreg; @rizza1979].

**Why this whole apparatus exists** is the key point. Almost every tissue that *can* switch to
burning fat does so in a fast — precisely so that the scarce glucose is left for the two consumers
that truly depend on it: the **brain** and the **red blood cell**. The red cell has **no
mitochondria**, so it can only run glycolysis and *must* have glucose. The brain is glucose-hungry
too — and here a widespread textbook line needs correcting: it is not that fatty acids "can't
cross into the brain," but that **fatty acids are a poor brain fuel** — they travel bound to
albumin and neurons oxidise them poorly [@schonfeld2013]. Everything else burning fat so these two
can have the glucose is called **glucose sparing.**

**The order of events** in a fast is therefore: **glycogenolysis first, gluconeogenesis second.**
Hepatic glycogen covers roughly the first **12–24 hours**; as it runs down, **gluconeogenesis**
takes over, building glucose from lactate, glycerol and glucogenic amino acids
[@statpearlsGlycogenolysis; @statpearlsGngPhys]. Meanwhile **lipolysis** — fat breakdown — becomes
the main fuel supply for everyone else: adipose releases **fatty acids** (for muscle, heart, liver)
and **glycerol** (which, being the one glucogenic part of fat, feeds gluconeogenesis)
[@statpearlsLipolysis].

::: {.callout-important}
## The centrepiece: muscle glycogen cannot raise your blood sugar
Muscle holds most of the body's glycogen — yet it cannot donate a single glucose molecule to the
blood. The reason is one missing enzyme: muscle lacks **glucose-6-phosphatase**, the enzyme that
frees glucose for export. So muscle glycogen is trapped for muscle's *own* use. **Only the liver
(and kidney) can release free glucose to the blood.** Muscle helps indirectly — by exporting
**lactate** (which the liver rebuilds into glucose: the *Cori cycle*) or **alanine** (which
carries both carbon and nitrogen to the liver: the *glucose–alanine cycle*). *The liver is the
gatekeeper of blood glucose; muscle only supplies raw material* [@statpearlsGlycogenolysis].
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cori
%%| fig-cap: "Muscle can't release glucose, so it sends carbon to the liver instead: lactate (Cori cycle) and alanine (which also carries nitrogen for disposal as urea)."
flowchart LR
  M["Muscle / red cell<br/>(no glucose export)"] -->|"lactate"| L["Liver<br/>makes glucose"]
  M -->|"alanine (carbon + nitrogen)"| L
  L -->|"glucose"| M
  L -.->|"nitrogen → urea"| U["Kidney<br/>excretes urea"]
```

## Prolonged fasting: ketones and the sparing of protein {#sec-starvation}

Keep fasting past a day, and the body faces a problem: it is defending blood glucose partly by
breaking down **protein** for gluconeogenesis — and protein is working tissue. Left unchecked, a
long fast would consume the body's own muscle. The elegant solution is **ketones**.

First, an accounting of what there is to spend — three very different accounts:

| Store | Rough size | Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Glycogen** | ~a day's worth of energy | The wallet — small, fast, first spent |
| **Fat** | ~weeks to months | The savings account — the vast bulk of stored energy |
| **Protein** | not a designed store at all | Functional tissue — spending it means borrowing against your own machinery |

(The exact figures vary by source and by how you count, so treat them as ranges. The important
shape is that fat dwarfs everything, glycogen is trivial by comparison, and protein is not really
"available" at all [@cahill2006fuel; @bergStryerFuel].)

**Fasting is a staged hand-off, not a switch** — often described as the *Cahill phases*: fed →
post-absorptive (glycogen) → early fasting (gluconeogenesis) → prolonged, protein-sparing ketosis
over days to weeks. And here is the payoff. As the fasting liver floods with acetyl-CoA from all
that fat breakdown, it makes **ketone bodies** — β-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate, and the exhaled
**acetone** — and exports them (recall it cannot use its own [@statpearlsKetogenesis]). Unlike
fatty acids, **ketones are water-soluble and cross into the brain freely.** Over days to weeks the
brain shifts the **majority** of its energy supply from glucose to ketones. Less glucose demanded
means less gluconeogenesis, which means **less protein broken down** — and survival stretches from
weeks toward months. This is the definitive correction to *"the brain can only use glucose."*

::: {.callout-warning}
## How solid are the starvation numbers?
The classic figures — the brain drawing well over half its energy from ketones, brain glucose use
falling roughly threefold — trace to a small, careful study of **three fasting obese volunteers**
(Owen and colleagues, 1967) and the body of work around it [@owen1967brain; @cahill2006fuel].
They are foundational and directionally right, but they are *not* modern large-trial constants.
Teach them as approximate trends: the brain's shift to ketones is real and large, but the precise
percentages rise gradually over days to weeks rather than snapping to a fixed value.
:::

The molecules the liver exports are simple. Rotate them below — note that "ketone bodies" is a
loose label (β-hydroxybutyrate is chemically a hydroxy-acid), and that **acetone** is the volatile
one you can smell on the breath of someone deep in ketosis or in the emergency next described.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/ketone-bodies.mol.yml"></div>

## Ketosis is not ketoacidosis {#sec-dka}

This is the myth-correction that matters most, because the words sound alike and mean very
different things.

- **Physiological (nutritional) ketosis** — what happens in a fast or on a ketogenic diet. It is
  **controlled by the residual insulin** you are still making: ketones rise modestly (roughly
  0.5–3 mmol/L on a keto diet, higher in prolonged starvation), and **blood pH stays normal.**
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)** — a medical emergency, seen when insulin is **almost entirely
  absent** (classically in type 1 diabetes). With no insulin to restrain them, fat breakdown and
  ketone production run away completely, the blood turns acidic, and the person is usually very
  hyperglycaemic as well.

::: {.callout-important}
## The discriminator is the acidosis, not the ketone number
It is natural to assume DKA is "just very high ketones," but the ketone ranges of deep nutritional
ketosis and mild DKA actually **overlap**. What defines DKA is the **acidosis** — an acidic blood
pH with a high anion gap — driven by uncontrolled insulin deficiency, not the ketone concentration
alone [@kolb2021ketones; @sheikhali2008bhb]. *Same molecules; the difference is regulatory context
and blood pH.* A healthy person on a keto diet does not get DKA, because they still make insulin.
(Recognising DKA is a first-year skill; managing it is Chapter 15.)
:::

## Refeeding: the danger of coming back too fast {#sec-refeeding}

One recognition-level hazard closes the starvation story. When a severely malnourished or starved
person is fed again, the sudden **insulin surge** drives **phosphate, potassium and magnesium
rapidly into cells**, and blood levels of these can crash — most dangerously **phosphate**
(hypophosphataemia) — with potentially fatal consequences. This is **refeeding syndrome**. For a
first-year the task is only to **recognise the at-risk patient** (the long-starved, the very
underweight, the person who has eaten little for many days) and know that refeeding must be slow
and monitored — the management is clinical-years work [@statpearlsRefeeding; @niceCg32].

## Reading which fuel is burning: the respiratory quotient {#sec-rq}

You can tell which fuel someone is burning from the gases they exchange. The **respiratory
quotient (RQ)** — CO₂ produced ÷ O₂ consumed — is about **1.0 when burning pure carbohydrate** and
about **0.7 when burning pure fat** (protein and a mixed diet sit in between, around 0.8–0.85).
Chapter 2 *defined* RQ; here we simply *read* it. After a carbohydrate meal, RQ climbs toward
1.0; deep into a fast it drifts toward 0.7 as the body switches to fat [@rqValuesStd]. The
metabolic-switch interactive above shows this drift as you step from fed to fasted — it is the map
telling you, in one number, which way the traffic is running.

## Fuel for exercise: hitting the wall {#sec-exercise}

Exercise is fuel selection under stress, and it is a continuum set by **intensity and duration**.
The very first seconds run on stored **phosphocreatine**; short, hard efforts run on **muscle
glycogen** (anaerobically, making lactate); lower-intensity, sustained effort draws increasingly
on **fat**. The **crossover concept** captures the trade: *as intensity rises, the fuel mix shifts
from fat toward carbohydrate* [@brooks1994crossover]. And because glycogen is the limited store,
**running it dry is "hitting the wall"** — the sudden fade a marathoner feels near 30 km, when the
glycogen tank empties and only slower fat oxidation is left.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The "fat-burning zone" myth
Gym machines advertise a low-intensity "fat-burning zone," and it is half true and wholly
misleading. Gentle exercise does burn a higher *proportion* of fat — but a smaller *total* number
of calories. Harder exercise burns more total energy, and more total fat, per minute. For losing
fat over weeks, what counts is the total energy deficit, not which zone a session sat in. Burning
"a higher percentage of fat" is not the same as burning more fat.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 From physiology to the plate: fuelling everyday exercise
What and when to eat around exercise follows straight from the map. Because muscle glycogen is a
*limited* store, topping it up matters — but only when you actually run it low. For everyday activity
(a run, a gym session, a social game), your existing glycogen is ample and **water is usually all you
need** — the sports drinks and gels are marketing, not physiology. Carbohydrate loading before, and
fuelling during, genuinely help only for **prolonged, hard** efforts lasting well beyond an hour,
where they delay "the wall" — the reason a marathoner carbo-loads and takes gels while a Sunday 5 km
runner does not.
:::

## Lipoproteins, interconverted {#sec-lipoproteins}

The last piece of integration is how fat travels in the blood — and it ties three chapters
together. Chapter 3 named the lipoprotein *classes*; Chapter 5 assembled the chylomicron in the
gut. Here they are simply a **pathway that interconverts.** Dietary fat leaves the gut as
**chylomicrons**; lipoprotein lipase strips their triglyceride, leaving **remnants** the liver
clears. The liver exports its own fat as **VLDL**, which sheds triglyceride to become **IDL** and
then **LDL** (the main cholesterol-delivery particle). And **HDL** runs the opposite errand —
reverse cholesterol transport, carrying cholesterol back from the tissues [@feingoldLipoproteins].

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-lipoproteins
%%| fig-cap: "Lipoproteins are not fixed types but a pathway that interconverts as they deliver and retrieve fat and cholesterol."
flowchart LR
  CM["Chylomicron<br/>(dietary fat, from gut)"] -->|"LPL strips triglyceride"| REM["Remnant"] --> LIV["Liver"]
  LIV -->|"exports"| VLDL["VLDL"] -->|"sheds triglyceride"| IDL["IDL"] --> LDL["LDL<br/>(delivers cholesterol)"]
  HDL["HDL"] -.->|"reverse transport"| LIV
```

---

## When the switch breaks {#sec-broken}

Understanding the healthy switch makes its failures legible. Three, at recognition level only:

**Diabetes is the switch, broken.** When insulin is missing or ignored, fed/fasted integration
fails: glucose cannot be stored or taken up by muscle and fat, so it piles up in the blood even
as the cells "think" they are starving. In **type 1**, the total loss of insulin's brake on
lipolysis lets fat breakdown and hepatic ketogenesis run unchecked — the road to **DKA** above.
The type 1/type 2 distinction and all management are Chapter 15; here, recognise the broken switch
[@ada2024care].

**Alcohol on an empty stomach → hypoglycaemia.** A clean, and clinically real, integration
failure. Metabolising ethanol floods the liver with a particular electron-carrier (NADH), and that
**blocks gluconeogenesis.** If glycogen is *also* low — say, after skipped meals and a night of
drinking — the liver can make glucose by neither route, and blood sugar can fall dangerously
[@krebs1969alcohol]. This is why heavy drinking without eating can end in a hypoglycaemic collapse
that looks, deceptively, "just drunk."

**Recognising a hypo.** When blood glucose drops, the counter-regulatory response produces a
telling order of symptoms: **adrenergic/autonomic warnings first** — tremor, sweating,
palpitations, hunger (driven by adrenaline, and appearing at a *higher* glucose level as an early
alarm) — then **neuroglycopenic** symptoms as the brain itself is starved of fuel: confusion,
slurred speech, eventually loss of consciousness [@mitrakou1991; @diabetologia2022thresholds]. The
reliable teaching point is *hormone/autonomic warning before the brain fails*; the fine ordering
of one symptom against another is less consistent than older teaching implied, and where exactly
the thresholds sit shifts with context (something Chapter 15 develops).

## The Singapore picture {#sec-singapore}

Metabolism is universal, but **when, what and how much we eat is cultural** — and Singapore's food
environment writes the fed/fasted switch in a particular way. This is where the chapter's physiology
becomes most practical.

**A culture of abundance keeps the switch storing.** Hawker centres open from breakfast to supper,
a strong snacking and late-night eating habit, sweet drinks by default, and a year-round calendar of
festivals mean many people spend a large share of the day in the **fed (storing) state**. The festive
peaks make it vivid: Chinese New Year, Hari Raya, Deepavali and Christmas each bring days of feasting
— reunion dinners, *bak kwa* and pineapple tarts, *ketupat* and *rendang*, log cakes and soft drinks
— a cultural over-supply that the fed-state machinery dutifully stores as glycogen and fat. Nothing is
metabolically "wrong"; the switch is doing exactly its job. But holiday weight gain is real, modest
per season, and — the part that matters — **not fully reversed afterwards, so it accumulates across
the years** [@yanovski2000holiday]. A food culture of frequent abundance simply keeps the storage side
of the switch busy.

**Ramadan — the fed/fasted switch, lived.** For a large part of the community the fed→fasted→refed
cycle is enacted daily for a month: no food or drink from dawn to dusk (~13–14 hours), with a
pre-dawn meal (*suhoor*) and breaking the fast at sunset (*buka puasa*). Physiologically each day runs
glycogenolysis giving way to gluconeogenesis with a **shift toward fat oxidation** — but, to be
honest about the depth, **not** dramatic afternoon ketosis; fasting blood glucose stays within the
normal range across the month [@lessan2019ramadan; @madkour2023metabolomics]. The food-first,
practical points fall straight out of the chapter: a *suhoor* built on **slower-digesting foods**
(wholegrains, protein, some fat) sustains the fast better than a sugary one, and the commonest pitfall
is the **rebound** — breaking a day's fast with a huge, sweet, fast-digesting spread. Eating to a
normal-sized meal at *buka puasa* and rehydrating overnight keeps the day's swing gentle.

For someone with diabetes on insulin or a sulfonylurea, this cultural practice becomes a genuine
clinical issue — one to **recognise and refer**, not manage. A long daily fast on glucose-lowering
medication markedly raises the risk of **hypoglycaemia**: a 13-country survey put the rise in severe
hypos at several-fold [@salti2004epidiar], and a Singapore study found nearly **half** of Muslim
patients with type 2 diabetes had a hypoglycaemic episode around Ramadan — many continuing to fast
without self-monitoring [@zainudin2017knowledge]. The evidence-based response is local too: the
Singapore-developed **FAST algorithm**, tested in a randomised trial, guides pre-Ramadan medication
adjustment [@lum2020fast]. The first-year task is to recognise the risk, advise breaking the fast for
a hypo, and refer for a pre-Ramadan review — management is Chapter 15.

**Supper and drinking culture.** The local habit of late-night drinking, often after a light dinner or
on an empty stomach, is exactly the setting for the **alcohol-and-fasting hypoglycaemia** met above —
which is why the plain advice *eat when you drink* is worth giving, and why a friend found confused
after a night out deserves a glucose check, not the assumption that they are "just drunk."

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 The honest state of intermittent fasting and keto
Both are popular here, and both work — but through less exotic mechanisms than the hype claims.
**Time-restricted eating** ("16:8" and the like) produces weight loss mostly by **cutting total
calories**, and in a careful trial was **not superior** to plain daily calorie restriction
[@liu2022tre; @decabo2019fasting]. The **ketogenic diet** likewise works largely through appetite and
calorie control — and, to close the loop, its nutritional ketosis is **not** the dangerous
ketoacidosis a patient may fear. The physiology is real; the magic is not. (Chapter 13 develops how to
weigh claims like these.)
:::

**Why this chapter matters here.** Singapore's **"War on Diabetes"** (declared 2016) sits on top of
all of this — roughly one resident in twelve lives with diabetes, and more in some communities
[@mohWarOnDiabetes]. A food culture that keeps the fed side of the switch busy, together with the
switch's own failure modes, is the physiology underneath that public-health effort and the
counselling and management skills of the later chapters.

## So — is fasting good or bad for you? {#sec-close}

The honest answer is that the question is the wrong shape. Your body **fasts every night** and
handles it beautifully: it banks glucose after dinner, spends the glycogen overnight, makes a
little glucose towards morning, and protects your brain the whole time — which is why your fasting
blood sugar is normal even though you "haven't eaten for twelve hours." Push the fast longer and
the same machinery escalates gracefully into ketosis, sparing your muscle for weeks. There is no
metabolic catastrophe in a missed meal, and no metabolic magic in a fasting schedule. What there
*is* — the thing this chapter has really been about — is a single, elegant switch: **store when
fed, mobilise when fasted, and defend the brain above all.** Learn to read it, and you can predict
what almost any nutritional state will do to the body.

## Check your understanding {#sec-quiz}

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/integrative-metabolism.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "he skipped meals, drank, and collapsed" {#sec-case}

A young man is brought to the clinic confused and sweating after an evening of heavy drinking on
an empty stomach. Work through why his blood sugar crashed — and why his liver could not rescue
him.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/alcohol-fasting-hypoglycaemia.case.yml"></div>
